reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear two-surface structure (Console vs CLI) with an explicit FeatureComparisonTable — exactly the kind of decision support a first-time user needs before reaching for the CLI.
Shell commands are clean: one command per fenced block, prose introduces each variant before the block, no explanatory # comments inside blocks.
Per-resource metric tables explain what each metric actually tells you (not just its name), which is valuable for a low-AWS-experience reader.
FAQ is comprehensive, spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, comparison/choosing), and answers are concise enough to function as featured snippets.
The Tip callout for choosing between Sum vs Average vs p99 is well-placed and saves a first-time user from a common mistake.
Resource-type coverage in both the comparison table and the per-section tables is thorough and correctly distinguishes Console (9 types) from CLI (4 types), consistent with the source.
problems: The time range preset table (aggregation periods: 60 s, 5 min, 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 6 h) is sourced from MetricsToolbar.tsx / getTimeRangeConfig, which is NOT in the provided source files. These specific numbers are ungrounded and may be incorrect.

Specific metrics shown by each Console chart (e.g., Lambda charts show 'Invocations, Duration, Errors'; RDS shows 'CPU, FreeMemory, DatabaseConnections, FreeStorage') are claimed from individual chart components (ChartLambda, ChartRds, etc.) whose source files are not provided. Making specific per-chart claims without the chart source is a source-grounding risk.

The claim 'Web services additionally show API Gateway metrics (requests, 5xx errors, latency) because Stacktape routes their traffic through an API Gateway' is an architectural claim not derivable from the provided source. CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx shows that ecs and apigateway are separate chart types, but does not assert that web-service resources produce both chart types simultaneously.

No navigation guidance for a first-time Console user. The page says 'Charts appear automatically on the stage details page' without explaining how to reach the metrics dashboard. A user who has just deployed their first stack will not know where to look.
mandatoryFixes: Caveat or remove the aggregation-period column from the time range preset table, or explicitly note that periods shown are approximate — the MetricsToolbar source file that defines getTimeRangeConfig is not in the provided source list and the exact values cannot be confirmed from available files.

Remove or soften the claim that web services show API Gateway metrics alongside ECS metrics. The CombinedMetricsGrid source lists ecs and apigateway as independent chart types; it does not assert that a single web-service resource maps to both. Replace with a factual description grounded in what the source shows.

Narrow the Console per-resource metric tables (Lambda shows Invocations/Duration/Errors, RDS shows CPU/FreeMemory/DatabaseConnections/FreeStorage, etc.) to only what can be verified from the provided source. The chart components (ChartLambda, ChartRds, ChartEcs, etc.) are referenced but not provided — specific metric names from those components are ungrounded. Either mark them as approximate or qualify the section with 'exact charts subject to Console version'.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence navigation hint for first-time users: e.g. 'Open the Stacktape Console, select your project, click the stage name, and scroll to the Metrics section.' This closes the most likely gap for a user who has just deployed their first stack.

The 'Basic usage' subsection of the CLI section could show the terminal output format (sparkline summary line with Min/Max/Avg/Sum) so users know what to expect before running the command — reduces uncertainty for first-timers.

The FAQ question 'What is the difference between Latency and IntegrationLatency for API Gateway?' is excellent but positioned late in the FAQ. Consider moving it earlier since it addresses a metric shown in the CLI examples on the same page.

Consider adding a brief note about the `--endTime` flag in the CLI section — the source shows it is supported but the docs only mention `--startTime`. Users running post-incident analysis will want to bound both ends of the window.

The CloudWatch data retention paragraph in the FAQ ('1-minute datapoints for 15 days, 5-minute for 63 days, 1-hour for 455 days') uses well-known AWS facts correctly, but adding a link to the AWS CloudWatch pricing/retention docs would let the reader verify current limits independently.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: CLI section is tightly grounded in `debug-metrics/index.ts`: default period=300, stat=Average, 1-hour default lookback, relative time units (h/m/d/s), and all four supported resource types with their exact metric names are accurate.

Console resource type count (9) is correctly derived from the `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx` component's `metric.type` switch — the 9 types (ecs, rds, apigateway, lambda, dynamodb, s3, cognito, redis, cloudfront) are all present as explicit chart component dispatches.

Shell command blocks are each on their own fenced block with prose introducing each variant — the multi-block time-range section is correctly split. No multi-command blocks with explanatory `#` comments.
The CLI vs Console comparison table (`FeatureComparisonTable`) is decision-useful and accurate: resource type counts, custom statistic support, and scriptable JSON output align with the source.
Default preset (`7d`) is confirmed in source (`DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d'`). The page correctly highlights this.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, and comparison questions — good breadth. The API Gateway Latency vs IntegrationLatency answer is technically precise and useful for debugging.
No mandatory style violations: no fenced YAML config blocks, no `StacktapeConfig` type import, no `defineConfig` in fenced TS blocks, no marketing language.
problems: The time-range preset table with specific aggregation periods (60s for 1h, 5min for 6h, 15min for 1d, 30min for 3d, 1h for 7d, 6h for 30d) is not grounded in any provided source file. `MetricsToolbar.tsx` is not in the source list — `getTimeRangeConfig` is imported but not shown. These numbers are plausible but unverified and could mislead production engineers reasoning about data resolution during incidents.

The per-resource Console chart tables (specific metric names like 'Free memory', 'Cache hits', 'Cache misses', 'Evictions', 'Network in/out', 'Current connections' for Redis; 'Database connections', 'Free storage' for RDS; 'Downloaded', 'Uploaded', '4xx errors' for CloudFront) are not verifiable from provided source. The `Chart*.tsx` component source files (ChartRedis, ChartRds, ChartCloudfront, etc.) are not provided. If these chart names are wrong, an engineer monitoring production would be misled about what they're looking at.

The claim 'Web services additionally show API Gateway metrics (requests, 5xx errors, latency) because Stacktape routes their traffic through an API Gateway' makes an architectural claim about web service internals not supported by any provided source file. The `CombinedMetricsGrid` confirms `apigateway` as a metric type, but the mapping from web-service resources to `apigateway` metric type is not shown.

The claim in the FAQ that 'Basic monitoring publishes metrics at 1-minute or 5-minute intervals depending on the service' and that metrics 'are covered by the free tier' for most services is presented as a Stacktape-contextual fact but is AWS pricing detail not found in any source file. It is broadly correct AWS knowledge, but the confident phrasing may mislead users on Detailed Monitoring costs for RDS (which is not free by default).
optionalImprovements: Add a caveat or omit the aggregation period column from the time-range preset table until `MetricsToolbar.tsx` source is available to confirm the values. Alternatively, label the column 'approximate' or 'typical'.

For the per-resource Console chart tables, either (a) caveat that the listed metric names are representative and actual chart labels may vary, or (b) limit Console chart documentation to the resource type names (which ARE source-grounded) and leave specific chart contents for users to discover in the Console. Remove unverifiable metric names like 'Cache hits', 'Cache misses', 'Evictions' from the Redis table until ChartRedis.tsx is confirmed.

The 'Web services additionally show API Gateway metrics' sentence should be qualified ('if the web service uses HTTP API Gateway for routing') or removed until the resource-type-to-metric-type mapping is confirmed from source.

The CLI supports a resource type named `function` internally (as shown in `METRIC_CONFIGS`) but users define it as the resource name in their config, not as the type string. The 'Supported resource types' table column lists display names like 'Lambda function' correctly but the parenthetical type strings (`function`, `multi-container-workload`, etc.) are internal values not typed by users. Clarify that `--resourceName` takes the config resource name (not the type string) to avoid confusion.

The FAQ answer about free-tier CloudWatch mentions 'Basic monitoring' vs 'Detailed monitoring' distinction. Consider adding that RDS Detailed Monitoring is a separate cost item (not the same as standard CloudWatch monitoring), since this is a common gotcha for teams scaling RDS usage.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong opening structure with a clear answer-first paragraph, short H2 headings, and a useful Console-vs-CLI comparison table.
CLI-supported resource types and metric names match the provided debug-metrics source.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and do not include explanatory comments inside copyable commands.
The page clearly separates Console metrics from the `stacktape debug:metrics` CLI surface in most sections.
problems: Several broad claims are unsupported by the provided sources, especially "Every deployed resource emits CloudWatch metrics" and "no instrumentation or configuration required." The Console source only shows chart components for nine metric data types, and the CLI source only supports four resource types.

The page invents or over-specifies Console behavior not present in the provided Console source, including stage details placement, chart grouping details, filtering UX, exact time range presets, default preset explanation beyond `7d`, and screenshot behavior.

Many metric-by-resource tables list chart names, units, interpretation, and operational thresholds that are not grounded in the provided source files because the chart component implementations were not included.

The CLI required-flags statement is too broad for the provided source. `debug-metrics` explicitly validates `--resourceName` and `--metric`; `--stage` and `--region` may be global command context, but that is not shown in the supplied source.

The `--stat` supported-values list is not fully grounded. The source passes `stat` through to CloudWatch and only establishes the default `Average`; it does not validate the listed values or special-case `p99`.

The FAQ contains several unsupported or risky claims: CloudWatch pricing/free-tier details, metric retention windows, RDS connection counts, S3 48-hour delay, ECS OOM restart behavior, third-party metric stream guidance, and custom metric alarm support are not in the supplied sources.

The alarms section is thin for the human-maintained observability instructions: it does not reference config-time alarm definitions from `types/stacktape-config/alarms.d.ts` or runtime Console alarm pages because those sources were not provided.

The Console-vs-CLI table says the Console supports exactly nine resource types, which is inferable from imported chart components, but the prose later gives deeper chart-level details that are not inferable from `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx` alone.
mandatoryFixes: Narrow the opening paragraph to the sourced behavior: Stacktape exposes CloudWatch metrics in the Console for metric data types rendered by the Console grid and through `stacktape debug:metrics` for the four CLI-supported resource types. Do not say every deployed resource emits surfaced metrics unless a source proves it.

Remove or cautiously rewrite unsupported Console UI specifics. Keep only what `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx` proves: default preset is `7d`, metrics can be filtered by `showOnlyForResource`, and chart components exist for ECS, RDS, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront.
Replace the metric-by-resource tables with source-grounded coverage, or require the relevant chart component source files before listing exact chart labels, units, and interpretations.

Correct the CLI flags section to say the command source explicitly validates `--resourceName` and `--metric`; only describe `--stage` and `--region` if supported by provided CLI initialization/global argument sources.

Rewrite the statistic section to say `--period` defaults to `300` seconds and `--stat` defaults to `Average`; avoid claiming an exhaustive supported list unless sourced from CLI docs or validation code.

Remove or soften FAQ claims that depend on AWS pricing, retention, S3 delay, ECS restart behavior, RDS connection limits, metric streams, or custom metric alarms unless those claims are backed by provided source files or intentionally allowed as general AWS background with careful AWS-side phrasing.
Revise the alarms section to either use provided alarm config and Console sources, or keep it narrow: metrics can be paired with alarms only as far as the supplied sources prove.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact table near the top showing the two sourced surfaces: Console metric grid vs `stacktape debug:metrics`, with scope and best-use guidance.
Add a short troubleshooting section for CLI errors that are directly sourced: missing `--resourceName`, missing `--metric`, unsupported resource type, and invalid metric name.
Replace the screenshot block if the referenced image is not guaranteed to exist in the docs repo, or keep the page useful without depending on the screenshot.
Make each FAQ answer name the relevant surface explicitly: "Stacktape Console metrics" or "the `stacktape debug:metrics` command," rather than generic "metrics" when behavior differs.

verifierResults.summary: The draft correctly captures several debug:metrics CLI details, but it overstates Console metric coverage and includes several precise Console/dashboard claims that are not grounded in the provided Console source. The highest-impact fixes are to narrow “every resource” language, avoid unsupported chart/time-range specifics unless the relevant component sources are included, and correct the CLI statistic aliases.

issues.statement: “Metrics are collected automatically — no instrumentation or configuration required. Every deployed resource emits CloudWatch metrics that track throughput, latency, errors, and resource utilization.” Similar wording appears again in the FAQ.
issues.reason: The provided sources only ground metrics surfaces for a limited set of Console metric types and four CLI resource types. They do not support the claim that every deployed Stacktape resource emits metrics, or that every deployed resource tracks throughput, latency, errors, and utilization.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the opening and FAQ to say Stacktape surfaces CloudWatch metrics for supported deployed resources. Name the Console-supported metric categories from the provided Console source and the CLI-supported resource types from the command source.

issues.statement: The Console vs CLI table says CLI custom statistics are “Sum, Average, Max, Min, p99”.
issues.reason: The command passes the --stat value directly to CloudWatch and does not translate aliases such as Max or Min. Elsewhere the draft uses the CloudWatch-style names Maximum and Minimum, which are the safer documented strings to show.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace “Max, Min” with “Maximum, Minimum”. If describing supported values, phrase them as CloudWatch statistic strings accepted through --stat rather than Stacktape-specific aliases.

evidence.quote: const { resourceName, metric, period = 300, stat = 'Average' } = args;

issues.statement: “The command requires four flags: --stage, --region, --resourceName, and --metric.”
issues.reason: The debug:metrics command source explicitly checks only --resourceName and --metric as required flags. The provided source does not show debug:metrics itself requiring --stage or --region, even though examples may reasonably include them for targeting a deployed stack.
issues.suggestedFix: Say the command requires --resourceName and --metric, and show --stage and --region in examples as target-selection flags unless global CLI source proving they are required is included.

issues.statement: The Console metrics dashboard section lists six exact time range presets and aggregation periods: 1h/60s, 6h/5m, 1d/15m, 3d/30m, 7d/1h, and 30d/6h.
issues.reason: The provided Console source grounds the default preset as 7d and shows that time range configuration comes from getTimeRangeConfig, but it does not include MetricsToolbar or the preset-to-period mapping. The exact preset list and aggregation periods are therefore not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded default 7d claim, and either remove the exact table or include the MetricsToolbar source that defines TimeRangePreset and getTimeRangeConfig.

evidence.quote: import { getTimeRangeConfig, MetricsToolbar } from './MetricsToolbar';
const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';

issues.statement: The “Metrics by resource type” section lists exact Console chart names, units, and interpretations for Lambda, ECS, RDS, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, Cognito, and Redis.
issues.reason: The provided Console source only shows that chart components are rendered for those metric categories. It does not include the chart component implementations, metric names, units, or chart labels, so the exact rows in those tables are not grounded in the source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the ChartLambda, ChartEcs, ChartRds, ChartApiGateway, ChartDynamoDb, ChartS3Bucket, ChartCloudfront, ChartCognitoUserPool, and ChartRedis source files, or narrow this section to the resource categories that the Console renders without listing exact chart rows.

issues.statement: “A web service shows both its ECS container metrics (CPU, memory) and its API Gateway metrics (requests, errors, latency) side by side” and “Web services additionally show API Gateway metrics because Stacktape routes their traffic through an API Gateway.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source renders chart components by metric.type, but it does not show how Stacktape resource types map to those metric types. The source does not prove that a web-service resource gets both ECS and API Gateway charts, or that web service traffic is routed through API Gateway.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the web-service-specific mapping unless getMetricsData or resource-type source is included. A safer grounded sentence is that the Console can render ECS and API Gateway metric charts when those metric entries are present for a stack.

evidence.quote: const metricsData = getMetricsData({ resources, stackInfoMap });

issues.statement: “You can also create alarms on custom metrics by referencing them in your Stacktape config.”
issues.reason: The provided source list does not include alarms configuration types or Console alarm pages. This may be true elsewhere, but it is not verifiable from the files provided for this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the relevant alarms source files and ground the custom-metric alarm syntax, or narrow the FAQ answer to say custom metrics are outside Stacktape’s built-in metrics surface and link to /observability/alarms for alarm configuration.

positiveFindings: The CLI-supported resource types and metric names in the draft match METRIC_CONFIGS for function, multi-container-workload, relational-database, and http-api-gateway.
The CLI defaults for --period 300, --stat Average, and default start time of one hour ago are grounded in commandDebugMetrics.
The draft’s distinction between terminal chart output and JSON output in agent mode matches the isAgentMode branch in the CLI source.
The Console default time range preset of 7d and filtering by showOnlyForResource are grounded in CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx.
All cross-links checked against the provided navigation index point to existing routes.

verifierResults.summary: The draft correctly distinguishes Console metrics from the `stacktape debug:metrics` CLI in broad terms, and the CLI-specific resource and metric list mostly matches the command source. The main risks are overbroad Console coverage claims, unsupported Console chart details, and a few CLI/statistic details that go beyond the provided command implementation.

issues.statement: “Every deployed resource emits CloudWatch metrics…” and “The Stacktape Console displays pre-configured metric charts for every resource in your stack.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source only renders metric chart components for a fixed set of metric data types. It does not support the claim that every deployed Stacktape resource has metrics in the Console or emits the listed metric categories through Stacktape.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to the resource types visible in the Console metrics component: Lambda, ECS, RDS, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront. Avoid saying “every deployed resource.”

issues.statement: “If you need metrics for other resource types (DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, etc.), use the Console dashboard which supports a broader set of resources.”
issues.reason: DynamoDB, S3, and CloudFront are supported by the Console source, but the phrase “other resource types” implies additional resource types beyond the listed Console chart types. The provided source does not support broader open-ended Console coverage.
issues.suggestedFix: Write that the Console supports additional chart types visible in source, such as DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront. Do not imply support beyond those types unless another source lists it.

issues.statement: The Console time range table lists six presets with exact durations and aggregation periods: 1h/60s, 6h/5m, 1d/15m, 3d/30m, 7d/1h, and 30d/6h.
issues.reason: The provided source confirms only that the default preset is `7d` and that time range settings come from `getTimeRangeConfig`. The actual preset list, durations, and period values are defined in `MetricsToolbar`, which is referenced but not included in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep only “the default Console preset is 7d” unless `MetricsToolbar.tsx` is included as source. If the preset table is needed, add the toolbar source to the source list and verify each value.

evidence.quote: import type { TimeRangePreset } from './MetricsToolbar';
import { getTimeRangeConfig, MetricsToolbar } from './MetricsToolbar';
const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';

issues.statement: “The Console groups charts by resource, so a web service shows both its ECS container metrics (CPU, memory) and its API Gateway metrics (requests, errors, latency) side by side.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source renders charts from `metricsData`, but it does not show the mapping from a Stacktape `web-service` to ECS and API Gateway chart entries. The claim may be true, but the included source does not prove this resource-specific behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the web-service-specific example or phrase it cautiously: “The Console renders chart groups returned by the stack data parser, including ECS and API Gateway chart types when present.”

evidence.quote: const metricsData = getMetricsData({ resources, stackInfoMap });

{filteredMetrics.map((metric, idx) => (
          <Fragment key={`${metric.stpResourceName}-${idx}`}>

issues.statement: “The `--stat` flag selects the statistic: `Sum`, `Average`, `Maximum`, `Minimum`, or `p99`.”
issues.reason: The command source accepts `stat` as a string and forwards it to CloudWatch with default `Average`. It does not enumerate or validate the allowed statistic values, so the draft’s exact supported-value list is not grounded in the provided Stacktape source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that `--stat` is passed as the CloudWatch statistic and defaults to `Average`. If examples use `Maximum`, keep that as an example rather than a complete Stacktape-supported list.

evidence.quote: const { resourceName, metric, period = 300, stat = 'Average' } = args;

Period: period,
      Stat: stat

issues.statement: “When running in agent mode (AI coding assistants, CI scripts), the output switches to structured JSON…”
issues.reason: The source confirms JSON output when `isAgentMode()` is true, but it does not define agent mode as including AI coding assistants or CI scripts. The parenthetical expands runtime behavior beyond the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write “When Stacktape detects agent mode, the command prints structured JSON…” and omit the parenthetical unless the agent-mode source is provided.

issues.statement: “Each resource type emits a different set of CloudWatch metrics. The tables below list what the Console charts for each resource type,” followed by exact chart names, units, and interpretations for Lambda, ECS, RDS, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, Cognito, and Redis.
issues.reason: The provided Console grid source proves that chart components exist for those metric data types, but it does not expose each chart’s metric names, units, or interpretation text. Those details likely live in the individual `Chart*.tsx` files, which are imported but not included.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove exact Console chart tables or add the relevant `ChartLambda`, `ChartEcs`, `ChartRds`, `ChartApiGateway`, `ChartDynamoDb`, `ChartS3Bucket`, `ChartCloudfront`, `ChartCognitoUserPool`, and `ChartRedis` sources before documenting chart-by-chart details.

issues.statement: The draft uses `<ConsoleScreenshot>` in the page body.
issues.reason: The verifier prompt asks to flag MDX component sanity issues when a component tag does not appear in the available component list, but no available MDX component list was provided in this prompt. The component may be valid elsewhere, but it is not verifiable from the supplied sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Confirm that `<ConsoleScreenshot>` is registered in the docs MDX component surface. If not, replace it with an available image/screenshot component or ordinary markdown image syntax.

positiveFindings: The draft’s CLI supported resource types and per-resource metric names match the `METRIC_CONFIGS` object in `src/commands/debug-metrics/index.ts`.
The CLI defaults for `--period` as 300 seconds, `--stat` as `Average`, and default start time as one hour before the end time are grounded in the command source.
The draft correctly links `stacktape debug:metrics` to `/cli/debug-metrics`, which exists in the provided documentation navigation index.
The Console-vs-CLI separation is directionally correct: the source shows a Console metrics grid and a distinct `debug:metrics` CLI command implementation.

verifierResults.summary: The page covers the main Console and CLI surfaces and accurately names the CLI-supported resource types and metric lists from the provided command source. The main completeness gap is the CLI `--endTime` option, which is implemented but not documented; the rest of the gaps are mostly unsupported precision rather than API-completeness misses.

issues.statement: The CLI time-range section documents `--startTime` but does not mention `--endTime`.
issues.reason: `commandDebugMetrics` accepts `endTime` and uses it to bound the CloudWatch query. Omitting it makes historical-window queries look start-only, even though users can set both ends of the range.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence and example explaining that `--endTime` can be set to an ISO timestamp; when omitted, the command uses the current time.

evidence.quote: endTime?: string;
const endTime = args.endTime ? new Date(args.endTime) : new Date();

issues.statement: “The command requires four flags: `--stage`, `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--metric`. ”
issues.reason: The provided command source explicitly validates only `--resourceName` and `--metric`. Stage and region may be required by shared initialization or CLI routing, but that requirement is not visible in the supplied source file, so the page should avoid presenting all four as command-local required flags unless supported elsewhere.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: “The command validates `--resourceName` and `--metric`; in normal deployed-stack commands you also pass the target stage and AWS region.”

issues.statement: “Six time range presets are available” with exact durations and aggregation periods.
issues.reason: The supplied Console source proves the default preset is `7d` and that presets come from `MetricsToolbar`, but it does not include the `MetricsToolbar` implementation or `getTimeRangeConfig` values. The exact preset list and aggregation periods are therefore not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide `MetricsToolbar.tsx` as a source file, or soften the section to only state the grounded behavior: the Console uses a preset toolbar, defaults to `7d`, and derives start time, end time, and period from the selected preset.

evidence.quote: const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';
const config = getTimeRangeConfig(preset);

issues.statement: “Charts appear automatically on the stage details page.”
issues.reason: The supplied Console source shows a `DeployedStageMetrics` component that renders a toolbar and metric grid from `useFullStackData`, but it does not show routing or page placement. The claim may be true, but this source file alone does not ground the exact Console location.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously: “The Console metrics component renders pre-configured charts for resources in the selected stack data.” If page placement is important, include the route/page source that mounts this component.

evidence.quote: export function Component({ showOnlyForResource }: { showOnlyForResource?: string }) {
Component.displayName = 'DeployedStageMetrics';

issues.statement: “The Console dashboard renders pre-configured charts for nine resource types (Lambda, ECS, RDS, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, Cognito, Redis).”
issues.reason: The source proves the grid conditionally renders chart components for nine internal metric types, but it does not prove the exact public resource-type coverage implied by the FAQ and comparison table. For example, `ecs` is an internal metric type, not necessarily a single user-facing resource type.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase this as internal metric families instead of exact resource-type count: “The Console renders chart families for ECS, RDS, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront data when those metric entries are present.”

positiveFindings: The page clearly distinguishes the Console metrics surface from the `stacktape debug:metrics` CLI workflow.
The CLI-supported resource types and metric names match the `METRIC_CONFIGS` map in the provided command source.
There are no Stacktape configuration examples, so the page avoids the forbidden YAML, `StacktapeConfig`, and fenced TypeScript config patterns.
The page includes a practical Console-vs-CLI comparison, which is appropriate for an observability page with both UI and command-line surfaces.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Self-contained, precise opening paragraph that immediately answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing — ideal for AI engine extraction.

All CLI claims are tightly grounded in the source: metric names, resource types, default period (300), default stat (Average), default 1-hour start window, and relative time unit support (h/m/d/s) all match `src/commands/debug-metrics/index.ts` exactly.
One command per fenced bash block throughout — no combined alternative blocks, no explanatory # comments inside shell snippets.
Console vs CLI comparison table is genuinely decision-supporting: rows map to concrete differences (scope, resource coverage, custom statistic, scriptable output) rather than feature-list padding.

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (setup required? agent mode?), AWS service questions (CloudWatch costs, retention periods, custom metrics), and comparison questions (Lambda vs container metric visibility, metrics vs logs). 10 Q&As, none synthetic or redundant.
'Understanding key metrics' section explains causation ('rising Duration often signals a performance regression'), not just values — exactly what a developer skimming after an incident needs.

All nine Console chart types (lambda, ecs, rds, apigateway, dynamodb, s3, cognito, redis, cloudfront) are grounded in `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx`. Default preset '7d' grounded in `DEFAULT_PRESET` constant.
The 'web services / private services / worker services run on ECS and use the multi-container-workload metric type' clarification is high-value and easy to miss — surfaced explicitly.
problems: The time-range preset table (1h→60s, 6h→5m, 1d→15m, 3d→30m, 7d→1h, 30d→6h) lists specific aggregation periods derived from `MetricsToolbar.tsx` → `getTimeRangeConfig()`, which is imported in `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx` but NOT provided as a source file. These six period values cannot be verified from the provided source and are high-specificity claims that could be wrong.

The claim that agent mode is activated 'via the `--agent` flag' is not grounded in the provided source. `isAgentMode()` is called from `'../_utils/agent-mode'` but its implementation is not shown. The flag name is inferred, not confirmed.

The page does not tell the reader how to navigate to the Console metrics dashboard — no URL fragment, no breadcrumb hint, no 'open the stage details page for your project' instruction with a link to the Console. For a first-time user with low AWS experience, 'the Stacktape Console' is not enough orientation.

The `<ConsoleScreenshot>` component does not appear in any other documented page, referenced source file, or example. Its prop surface (src, alt, caption) looks plausible but the component name itself cannot be verified as part of the docs MDX component palette. If it doesn't exist in the system, the image block silently renders nothing.
mandatoryFixes: Verify or remove the aggregation-period column from the time-range preset table. The six period values (60s, 5m, 15m, 30m, 1h, 6h) come from `MetricsToolbar.tsx` which is not in the provided source files. Either supply that source file for grounding or replace the table with the six preset labels only, noting that granularity is chosen automatically by the Console.

Verify the `--agent` flag claim against the `isAgentMode()` implementation in `src/commands/_utils/agent-mode.ts`. If the flag is named differently or the mode is detected from an env var rather than a flag, correct the prose in the 'Output formats' subsection. If the source cannot be confirmed, narrow the wording to 'when Stacktape detects agent mode' without naming the flag.

Replace `<ConsoleScreenshot>` with a verified MDX component from the docs component palette (e.g. a standard `<Info>` callout pointing users to the Console, or simply remove the component call) if the component cannot be confirmed as valid. A broken component silently omits the image and leaves a gap the reader notices.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence navigation hint in the 'Console metrics dashboard' section: e.g. 'Open the Stacktape Console, select your project, pick a stage, and click the Metrics tab.' First-time users do not know where the dashboard lives within the Console flow.

The 'Using metrics with alarms' section is two sentences — it reads more like a cross-link placeholder than a section. Either expand it with one concrete scenario (a Lambda error-count alarm config snippet) or fold the content into the FAQ and remove the H2.

In the 'Output formats' subsection, clarify what structured JSON looks like with a short inline example or at least describe the top-level keys (resource, metric, period, stat, unit, datapoints) — these are confirmed in the source and give AI assistants and CI scripts a concrete contract to work against.

The 'Filtering by resource' subsection under 'Console metrics dashboard' is accurate but thin — one paragraph with no example of what changes visually. A note like 'select the resource name from the filter dropdown in the toolbar' would close the interaction loop for first-time users who may not find the control.

The FAQ answer for 'How far back can I view metrics?' uses specific CloudWatch retention numbers (15 days, 63 days, 455 days). These are well-known AWS facts so no grounding concern, but they drift over time. Consider phrasing as 'AWS CloudWatch retains high-resolution data for up to 15 days and lower-resolution aggregates for longer periods; check AWS documentation for current retention limits.'

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: CLI metric names, defaults (period=300, stat=Average, startTime=1h), and supported resource types are all precisely source-grounded from debug-metrics/index.ts.
Relative time unit table (s/m/h/d) matches the regex in the source exactly — zero fabricated syntax.
Console vs CLI distinction is clear and section-level, satisfying the observability section requirement. The FeatureComparisonTable is an effective summary.
FAQ section is broad and high-quality: mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS behavioral, and comparison questions, with 10 distinct Q&A pairs.
Understanding key metrics section explains what rising/spiking values mean in practice, not just what the metric names are — genuinely useful for a production operator.
No Stacktape config examples — correctly omitted for an observability page with no resource Props type.
Each CLI example is a single command per fenced block; no multi-command copy-button traps.
problems: The time range preset table (1h/6h/1d/3d/7d/30d) with specific aggregation periods (60s, 300s, 900s, 1800s, 3600s, 21600s) is not grounded in the provided source. MetricsToolbar.tsx is imported in CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx but its contents were not provided. If these period values are wrong, a production user relying on them to understand metric resolution will be misled.

The claim that agent mode is triggered 'via the `--agent` flag' is not confirmed in the provided source. The source only shows isAgentMode() being called from an external utility — the specific flag name is not visible in the provided files.

The page asserts that batch jobs run on ECS and use the `multi-container-workload` metric type. The METRIC_CONFIGS object in the CLI source only has four keys (function, multi-container-workload, relational-database, http-api-gateway) and does not enumerate batch-job as an alias for multi-container-workload. This is plausible but unconfirmed from the provided source.

The default time range (last 1 hour) is correctly stated, but the page also presents detailed per-preset aggregation periods as authoritative facts. Presenting unverified precision may be worse than presenting no precision, since a user debugging with 6h thinking they're getting 5-minute granularity could be misled if the actual value differs.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or caveat the specific aggregation-period column from the time range preset table. The provided source files do not include MetricsToolbar.tsx, so the period values (60s, 300s, 900s, etc.) are ungrounded. Either omit the Aggregation period column entirely, or add a note that values are approximate and subject to CloudWatch data availability — do not present them as authoritative Stacktape-configured values.
Remove the '`--agent` flag' claim or replace with 'when Stacktape detects agent mode' without specifying the flag name, since isAgentMode() implementation is not in the provided source files.

Remove batch jobs from the list of resource types that use the `multi-container-workload` CLI metric type, or scope the claim to 'web services, private services, and worker services' only — those three sharing the ECS substrate is more defensible than asserting batch jobs do too without source confirmation.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-line note that the `--resourceName` value must match the resource's key in the Stacktape config exactly (case-sensitive), since a mismatch silently returns a 'resource not found' error — this is a common source of confusion when first using the CLI command.

In the 'Aggregation and statistics' section, note that very small `--period` values may return no data if CloudWatch hasn't retained metrics at that resolution for the requested time window — this is a common gotcha when combining a short period with a long time range.

The FAQ answer 'How far back can I view metrics?' states the CLI accepts '--startTime 60d'. This is plausible given the regex support for 'd' units, but CloudWatch's 15-month retention and the actual behavior with very old data could be worth a brief caveat rather than presenting it as fully tested behavior.

Consider adding a short note that the Console chart granularity is tied to the preset (coarser for longer presets) so users who want fine-grained recent data should use the CLI with a small --period rather than the 7d or 30d Console view.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and names both Stacktape Console and `stacktape debug:metrics`.
The Console vs CLI comparison table is high-signal and helps readers choose the right surface quickly.
CLI metric support is presented concretely with resource types, metric names, defaults for `--period`, `--stat`, and default time window grounded in the CLI source.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and are practical for terminal use.
FAQ has broad coverage across Stacktape behavior, CloudWatch use cases, and choosing metrics versus logs.
problems: Several Console details are not grounded in the provided Console source. The source shows `DEFAULT_PRESET: '7d'`, `MetricsToolbar`, and chart categories, but not the six preset labels, durations, aggregation periods, toolbar behavior, or dropdown UI.

The screenshot component and its alt/caption imply visible Console controls such as a resource filter dropdown, but the provided source only shows a `showOnlyForResource` prop and does not prove a dropdown exists in this component.
The CLI output description claims terminal summary statistics `Min`, `Max`, `Avg`, and `Sum`, but the provided source only calls `printMetricsChart`; the formatter source is not included.
The agent-mode description says agent mode is detected via `--agent`, but the provided source only calls `isAgentMode()` and does not define the triggering flag.

Some operational interpretations go beyond the source, such as sustained CPU above 80% suggesting more CPU/containers, memory hitting 100% causing OOM kills, and RDS connection limits being a few hundred by default. These may be useful AWS guidance, but they are not source-grounded Stacktape facts.
The FAQ answer on CloudWatch costs gives broad AWS pricing claims even though the observability instructions say cost is a separate section and cost content should not be included here.

The page states `No instrumentation or SDK setup is required`; that is likely true for AWS service metrics, but the provided source only proves Stacktape queries existing CloudWatch metrics, not the absence of all setup requirements.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow ungrounded Console preset details unless `MetricsToolbar.tsx` is added to the source set. Keep only the grounded `7d` default and chart-category behavior, or source the exact preset table.

Remove cost content from the Metrics page FAQ. Replace the CloudWatch pricing answer with a short pointer to `/managing-costs/overview` if needed, because the observability instructions say cost belongs in a separate section.

Replace unsupported CLI output claims with source-grounded wording, or add the formatter source. For now, say non-agent mode renders a chart via the terminal formatter and agent mode prints JSON with `resource`, `metric`, `period`, `stat`, `unit`, and `datapoints`.
Avoid asserting `--agent` as the mechanism for agent mode unless the agent-mode utility source is provided. Phrase cautiously: “In agent mode, the command prints structured JSON.”
Revise the screenshot/caption and filtering section so they do not claim a specific dropdown UI unless the Console source proving that UI is included.
optionalImprovements: Move the cost-related FAQ entry to a short “Where can I understand monitoring costs?” answer that links to `/managing-costs/overview` without making pricing claims.

Add a brief “Limits of built-in metrics” section explaining that `debug:metrics` supports only four resource types while the Console chart grid covers nine categories, since that is one of the most important decisions on the page.
Where giving AWS interpretation advice, label it as general operational guidance rather than Stacktape behavior, or remove exact thresholds unless sourced.
Consider shortening “CLI: stacktape debug:metrics” to “CLI metrics” for heading consistency, while keeping the linked command in the first paragraph of the section.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the two provided sources for the core split between Console metrics and `debug:metrics`. The main risks are several detailed Console time-range claims that are not grounded in the provided Console source, one overbroad statement about ECS-derived resource types using the CLI metric type, and a few unsupported CLI/output specifics that should be narrowed to the command source.

issues.statement: “Web services, private services, worker services, and batch jobs run on ECS and use the `multi-container-workload` metric type.”
issues.reason: The `debug:metrics` command supports only resources whose deployed `resourceType` is exactly `multi-container-workload`. The provided command source does not map `web-service`, `private-service`, `worker-service`, or `batch-job` to `multi-container-workload`; unsupported resource types throw an error.
issues.suggestedFix: Say only that the CLI supports deployed resources whose resource type is `multi-container-workload`. If you want to discuss web/private/worker services or batch jobs, limit that to Console ECS charts unless source proving their deployed `resourceType` is provided.

issues.statement: “The Console toolbar offers six time range presets” and the table listing `1h`, `6h`, `1d`, `3d`, `7d`, `30d` with specific aggregation periods.
issues.reason: The provided Console source confirms a toolbar, a default preset of `7d`, and that period seconds come from `getTimeRangeConfig`, but it does not include the `MetricsToolbar` implementation or the preset-to-period mapping. The six presets and aggregation periods are therefore not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the supported default claim (`7d`) and narrow the rest to “the toolbar changes the start time, end time, and CloudWatch period based on the selected preset,” or provide the `MetricsToolbar.tsx` source before documenting the exact presets and periods.

evidence.quote: import { getTimeRangeConfig, MetricsToolbar } from './MetricsToolbar';
const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';

issues.statement: “The CLI provides targeted metric queries for four resource types directly in your terminal.” / “The CLI currently supports four resource types.”
issues.reason: The command source defines four keys in `METRIC_CONFIGS`, but one key is `multi-container-workload`, not a family of all ECS resources. Calling them “resource types” is acceptable only if the table uses the exact deployed resource types from the source. The draft later broadens the ECS row to other Stacktape resources, which makes the count misleading.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase the CLI support as “four deployed resource type keys: `function`, `multi-container-workload`, `relational-database`, and `http-api-gateway`.” Avoid implying all Stacktape container resources are accepted unless source proves their deployed type key.

issues.statement: “The command requires two flags: `--resourceName` and `--metric`. You also need `--stage` and `--region` to identify the target stack.”
issues.reason: The command source explicitly throws missing-flag errors for `resourceName` and `metric`. The provided command source does not show equivalent required-flag checks for `stage` or `region`; those may be global CLI requirements, but they are not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Say the command itself validates `--resourceName` and `--metric`. If examples need `--stage` and `--region`, present them as target-stack selection flags rather than claiming this command requires them, unless command registration/global CLI source is provided.

issues.statement: “In a standard terminal, the command renders a sparkline chart with summary statistics (Min, Max, Avg, Sum) and the time range.”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms non-agent mode calls `printMetricsChart`, but it does not show what that formatter renders. The Min/Max/Avg/Sum and time range details are not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to “In non-agent mode, the command passes datapoints to Stacktape’s metrics chart formatter.” Only mention specific displayed summary fields if the formatter source is provided.

issues.statement: “The value is passed directly to the CloudWatch API, so any valid CloudWatch statistic string works.”
issues.reason: The source passes `stat` into `MetricStat.Stat`, but the source file does not validate or document the full accepted set of CloudWatch statistic strings. This is likely true as AWS background, but the wording is broad and may imply Stacktape intentionally supports every valid statistic form.
issues.suggestedFix: Use narrower wording: “Stacktape passes `--stat` to the CloudWatch `MetricStat.Stat` field. Common values include `Sum`, `Average`, `Maximum`, and `Minimum`.”

issues.statement: “The CLI accepts any `--startTime` value within CloudWatch's retention window.”
issues.reason: The command parses relative durations matching `^(\d+)([hmds])$` or otherwise passes the string into `new Date(args.startTime)`. The provided source does not validate that arbitrary values are within CloudWatch retention or describe behavior for invalid dates or too-old ranges.
issues.suggestedFix: Say the CLI accepts relative durations such as `30m` or `1d`, or an absolute timestamp parseable by JavaScript `Date`; CloudWatch determines what historical datapoints are available.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly identifies the nine Console chart categories shown by `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx`: ECS, RDS, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront.
The draft correctly documents the `debug:metrics` supported metric names for `function`, `multi-container-workload`, `relational-database`, and `http-api-gateway` as defined in `METRIC_CONFIGS`.
The draft correctly states the CLI defaults `period` to `300`, `stat` to `Average`, and the time window to the last hour when `--startTime` is omitted.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the two provided metrics sources for the main Console categories and the core `debug:metrics` CLI behavior. The main problems are over-specific Console preset/output details that are not present in the provided source, and one likely incorrect claim that other ECS-backed Stacktape resources can be queried through the CLI as `multi-container-workload`.

issues.statement: “Web services, private services, worker services, and batch jobs run on ECS and use the `multi-container-workload` metric type.”
issues.reason: The CLI source only configures metrics for resources whose deployed `resource.resourceType` is exactly one of `function`, `multi-container-workload`, `relational-database`, or `http-api-gateway`. The command does not map `web-service`, `private-service`, `worker-service`, or `batch-job` to `multi-container-workload`; unsupported resource types throw an error. This claim could lead readers to run unsupported CLI commands against those resource names.
issues.suggestedFix: Say only that the CLI supports deployed resources with resource type `multi-container-workload`. Do not claim web services, private services, worker services, or batch jobs can be queried through that CLI type unless a source file proves the mapping.

issues.statement: The Console has six time range presets with the exact durations and aggregation periods shown in the table: 1h/60s, 6h/5m, 1d/15m, 3d/30m, 7d/1h, and 30d/6h.
issues.reason: The provided Console source proves that a `7d` default exists and that time range configuration comes from `getTimeRangeConfig`, but it does not include the `MetricsToolbar` implementation or enumerate the supported presets and period values. The exact preset table is therefore not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded default `7d` claim, but remove the exact preset table unless the `MetricsToolbar` source is included and verifies the preset list and periods.

evidence.quote: import { getTimeRangeConfig, MetricsToolbar } from './MetricsToolbar';
const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';

issues.statement: “When Stacktape detects agent mode (via the `--agent` flag), the command prints structured JSON…”
issues.reason: The command source confirms that JSON is printed when `isAgentMode()` returns true, but the provided source does not show that agent mode is activated by a `--agent` flag. The flag name is therefore unsupported by the provided CLI source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write “When Stacktape detects agent mode, the command prints structured JSON…” and omit `--agent` unless the command/global CLI source is provided and confirms that flag.

evidence.quote: import { isAgentMode } from '../_utils/agent-mode';

issues.statement: “In a standard terminal, the command renders a sparkline chart with summary statistics (Min, Max, Avg, Sum) and the time range.”
issues.reason: The provided command source only shows that non-agent mode calls `printMetricsChart` with metric data. It does not show the implementation of `printMetricsChart`, so the specific claims about Min, Max, Avg, Sum, and time range output are not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to “In a standard terminal, the command renders a metrics chart.” Add the summary-stat details only if the formatter source is included and verifies them.

evidence.quote: import { printMetricsChart } from '../_utils/debug-formatters';

issues.statement: “Stacktape does not provide a built-in integration for forwarding metrics to third-party monitoring platforms.”
issues.reason: The provided metrics sources do not mention third-party metrics forwarding either way. The claim may be true, but it is a broad product capability statement that is not verifiable from the supplied Console metrics and `debug:metrics` command files.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase cautiously: “This page’s built-in metrics surfaces cover the Stacktape Console and `debug:metrics`; third-party metric forwarding is not documented here.”

issues.statement: “Custom metrics appear in the AWS CloudWatch Console and can be referenced when creating alarms in your Stacktape config.”
issues.reason: Publishing custom metrics to CloudWatch is general AWS behavior, but the Stacktape-specific claim that custom metrics can be referenced in Stacktape alarm config is not supported by the provided metrics sources. No alarm type source is included in the source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the AWS custom-metrics guidance, but remove or narrow the Stacktape alarm-config claim unless `types/stacktape-config/alarms.d.ts` or equivalent alarm source is provided.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly identifies the nine Console metric categories shown by `CombinedMetricsGrid.tsx`: ECS, RDS, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, Cognito, Redis, and CloudFront.
The draft correctly lists the four CLI-supported resource types and metric names from `METRIC_CONFIGS`.
The draft correctly describes the grounded CLI defaults for `period = 300`, `stat = 'Average'`, and the default one-hour time window when `--startTime` is omitted.
All markdown cross-links checked in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is strong on the CLI command because it closely follows src/commands/debug-metrics/index.ts. The main audit risk is that several Console-specific details are more precise than the provided Console source supports, especially time-range preset values and chart behavior. No primary config type is available, so there is no resource-property API completeness audit to perform.

issues.statement: The Console toolbar offers six time range presets with exact durations and aggregation periods: 1h/60s, 6h/5m, 1d/15m, 3d/30m, 7d/1h, 30d/6h.
issues.reason: The provided Console source proves that a MetricsToolbar exists, that DEFAULT_PRESET is '7d', and that getTimeRangeConfig returns durationMs and periodSeconds. It does not include MetricsToolbar.tsx, so the exact list of presets and period values is not grounded in the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add MetricsToolbar.tsx to the source set or narrow the section to only state that the Console uses a selected time-range preset, defaults to 7d, and passes the preset's duration and period into metric chart queries.

evidence.quote: import type { TimeRangePreset } from './MetricsToolbar';
const DEFAULT_PRESET: TimeRangePreset = '7d';
const config = getTimeRangeConfig(preset);

issues.statement: The Console renders metric charts for nine resource categories — Lambda, ECS, RDS, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, CloudFront, Cognito, and Redis. No instrumentation or SDK setup is required.
issues.reason: The source grounds the nine Console chart branches. It does not ground the broad operational claim that no instrumentation or SDK setup is required for every charted metric category. That may be true for AWS service metrics, but it is not established by the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the nine supported chart categories, but revise the setup claim to something directly grounded, such as: 'The Console builds these charts from deployed stack resource data and the corresponding metric chart components.' If you want the no-instrumentation claim, add source that shows how metrics are retrieved from CloudWatch without app instrumentation.

evidence.quote: {metric.type === 'ecs' && ChartEcs({ ...chartProps, ...metric })}
{metric.type === 'cloudfront' && ChartCloudfront({ ...chartProps, ...metric })}

issues.statement: Charts appear automatically and update based on the selected time range preset.
issues.reason: The selected time range behavior is grounded, but 'appear automatically' is broad. The source shows metricsData is derived from resources and stackInfoMap, filtered, and rendered. It does not show lifecycle behavior, permissions, empty-state behavior, or whether charts appear automatically in all deployed-stage contexts.
issues.suggestedFix: Use narrower wording: 'The metrics grid derives chart entries from deployed stack data and renders the matching chart component for each supported metric type. Changing the selected preset rebuilds the time range passed to each chart.'

evidence.quote: const metricsData = getMetricsData({ resources, stackInfoMap });
const handlePresetChange = (preset: TimeRangePreset) => { setSelectedPreset(preset); setTimeRange(buildTimeRange(preset)); };

issues.statement: Web services, private services, worker services, and batch jobs run on ECS and use the multi-container-workload metric type.
issues.reason: The CLI source only supports resource.resourceType === 'multi-container-workload' for ECS metrics. The supplied source does not prove that web-service, private-service, worker-service, or batch-job resources are represented as multi-container-workload in deployed stack overview data, nor that batch jobs use the same metric dimension.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add deployed stack overview/resource normalization source proving those mappings, or narrow the CLI section to the grounded resourceType value: '`debug:metrics` supports deployed resources whose type is `multi-container-workload` for ECS CPU and memory metrics.'

evidence.quote: 'multi-container-workload': { namespace: 'AWS/ECS', dimensionName: 'ServiceName', metrics: ['CPUUtilization', 'MemoryUtilization'] }
const resourceType = resource.resourceType as string; const metricConfig = METRIC_CONFIGS[resourceType];

issues.statement: The CLI prints structured JSON containing the full array of timestamped datapoints when Stacktape detects agent mode via the --agent flag.
issues.reason: The JSON output shape and use of isAgentMode are grounded. The provided command source does not show that --agent is the flag that activates agent mode; that behavior lives in ../_utils/agent-mode or CLI argument handling, which is not included.
issues.suggestedFix: Revise to: 'When `isAgentMode()` is true, the command prints structured JSON containing resource, metric, period, stat, unit, and timestamped datapoints.' Add the agent-mode source if you want to document the exact `--agent` flag.

evidence.quote: if (isAgentMode()) { tuiManager.info(JSON.stringify({ resource: resourceName, metric, period, stat, unit: results[0]?.Label || '', datapoints }, null, 2)); }

issues.statement: When Stacktape detects agent mode, JSON output is useful for programmatic consumption by AI coding assistants or CI scripts.
issues.reason: The source shows JSON output in agent mode, but does not document intended consumers such as AI coding assistants or CI scripts. This is product guidance rather than API behavior, so it needs supporting source or more cautious wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded output behavior and remove or soften the intended-consumer claim: 'This is easier for tools to parse than the terminal chart.'

evidence.quote: if (isAgentMode()) { tuiManager.info(JSON.stringify(...)); } else { printMetricsChart({ metric, resourceName, stat, period, datapoints }); }

issues.statement: The command renders a sparkline chart with summary statistics (Min, Max, Avg, Sum) and the time range.
issues.reason: The debug-metrics source calls printMetricsChart with metric, resourceName, stat, period, and datapoints, but the formatter implementation is not provided. The exact visual form, summary statistics, and displayed time range are not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the debug formatter source or narrow the statement to: 'Outside agent mode, the command passes the metric, resource name, statistic, period, and datapoints to the terminal metrics formatter.'

evidence.quote: printMetricsChart({ metric, resourceName, stat, period, datapoints });

issues.statement: The CLI currently supports four resource types. Each resource type has a fixed set of valid --metric values.
issues.reason: The table correctly names the four METRIC_CONFIGS entries and metric lists. The missing caveat is that the CloudWatch dimension used by the command is also fixed per resource type, and for relational databases and HTTP API Gateway the dimension value comes from referenceable params when available. That matters when explaining why a config resource name maps to the correct CloudWatch metric.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short sentence after the table: 'The command maps each Stacktape resource to a fixed CloudWatch namespace and dimension: Lambda FunctionName, ECS ServiceName, RDS DBInstanceIdentifier, and API Gateway ApiId. For RDS and API Gateway, Stacktape uses `instanceId` or `apiId` from the deployed resource parameters when available.'

evidence.quote: const METRIC_CONFIGS: Record<string, { namespace: string; dimensionName: string; metrics: string[] }> = {
dimensionValue = params?.instanceId?.value || `${stackName}-${resourceName}`;
dimensionValue = params?.apiId?.value || '';

positiveFindings: The CLI metric lists for function, multi-container-workload, relational-database, and http-api-gateway match METRIC_CONFIGS in src/commands/debug-metrics/index.ts.
The draft correctly states that --resourceName and --metric are required; both are explicitly validated by the command source.
The draft correctly documents the CLI defaults period = 300, stat = 'Average', and default time window of the last 1 hour.
The Console-vs-CLI distinction is directionally correct: the Console source renders nine chart categories, while the CLI command source supports four resourceType entries.
The draft avoids forbidden Stacktape config examples entirely; there are no hand-authored YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig typed examples, and no fenced TypeScript Stacktape config blocks.

pipelineReviewSummary: reviewer-avg 7.9, hard blockers 2, advisory high 0, verifier medium 15
